english: Indian vegetable fritters in chickpea batter

food_subgroup: Deep-fried spiced vegetable fritters in chickpea flour batter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Vegetable Pakora. Nearest parent is a deep-fried vegetable snack. A new concept for Indian pakora-style fritters is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried mixed vegetable fritters in legume batter (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for pakora; A03RJ used as closest parent for deep-fried legume-battered vegetable fritters.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Vegetable Pakora as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Vegetable Pakora is 6 pieces (approximately 150 g), as commonly served as a starter or snack portion at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Vegetable Pakora (besan-battered deep-fried vegetable data)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for besan-battered deep-fried vegetable preparations. Values represent a composite of mixed vegetable pakora as commonly prepared. All per_100g values are sourced from this reference with confidence 1.0 as reported in the input record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chickpea Flour-Based Deep-Fried Vegetable Fritters — analogous estimate from chickpea-battered preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 38 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database data for chickpea-battered deep-fried vegetable preparations. Chickpea flour (besan) is the primary batter ingredient and is well-documented to produce a low GI due to its high resistant starch and protein content. GL per 150 g serving calculated as (38 × 31.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 17.9, rounded to 17.8 as reported. Confidence 0.9 (analogous estimate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 285 mg per 100 g; approximately 428 mg per 150 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Vegetable Pakora to occasional consumption, as a 150 g serving contributes approximately 428 mg sodium (about 19% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and sodium intake is further increased when served with salted mint chutney or tamarind dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~38) due to chickpea flour (besan) batter; moderate glycemic load of approximately 17.8 per 150 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegetable Pakora has a low GI of approximately 38 attributable to the chickpea flour batter, making it a relatively favourable snack choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a standard 6-piece (150 g) portion, though the moderate glycemic load and deep-fried fat content warrant portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 17.5 g per 100 g from deep-frying; saturated fat 2.2 g per 100 g; cholesterol 0 mg (plant-based)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process contributes 17.5 g total fat per 100 g, of which 2.2 g is saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency, though the absence of cholesterol and the presence of dietary fibre (4.2 g per 100 g) from chickpea flour and vegetables provide some cardiovascular benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Pakora is a classic Indian deep-fried snack consisting of sliced or diced mixed vegetables — typically onion, potato, spinach, green chilli, and cauliflower — coated in a thick, spiced chickpea flour (besan) batter seasoned with ajwain (carom seeds), red chilli powder, ground coriander, and salt, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy. The chickpea flour batter creates a distinctive, slightly nutty, crispy coating that is naturally gluten-free and vegan. Pakoras are universally available at Indian restaurants in Singapore as a starter or snack, and are a staple of Indian street food culture across the subcontinent. Nutritionally, per 100 g, Vegetable Pakora provides approximately 295 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 17.5 g fat (2.2 g saturated), 31.5 g carbohydrate, 4.2 g dietary fibre, and 285 mg sodium. Despite the deep-frying, the chickpea flour base gives pakoras a surprisingly low glycaemic index of approximately 38, making them a relatively favourable snack for individuals managing blood glucose. The dietary fibre from both the chickpea flour and the mixed vegetables supports digestive health. Pakoras are best consumed fresh and hot, and are commonly served with mint-coriander chutney or tamarind sauce. In Singapore, they are a popular starter at Indian restaurants and are also found at hawker centres and Indian food stalls.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for besan-battered deep-fried vegetable preparations, which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes. The GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chickpea-battered preparations rather than a direct measurement of this specific dish. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and analogous GI estimate.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source for vegetable oil deep-frying without hydrogenated fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero for a plant-based deep-fried snack with no fortification). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for mixed vegetable pakora not reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this preparation not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. The ethnic_tag 'Middle Eastern' from the input record has been removed as Vegetable Pakora is an Indian dish with no established Middle Eastern origin. The alias '134. Mix Vegetable Pakora' appears to be a menu item number and has been retained for retrieval purposes but is not the canonical name. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database chickpea-battered preparations; direct GI measurement for this specific dish was not located. GL per serving calculated as (38 × 31.5 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 17.9, consistent with the input value of 17.8.
